Entertainment.title: Bonnie Tyler funeral to be broadcast online to fans
Entertainment.text:
Fans around the world will be able to watch a celebration of life service for Welsh singer Bonnie Tyler in an online broadcast.

The Total Eclipse of the Heart singer died "unexpectedly" on 8 July, aged 75, at a Portugal hospital, "as a result of the illness that she was being treated for", her family said.

The service for family and invited guests will be held at St Mary's Church, Swansea, on 17 August, and will be live streamed on the star's official website.

The cortege will then travel to her hometown of Skewen, Neath Port Talbot, travelling through the village at about 13:20 BST, an update on the website, external said.

Fans will also be able to watch the service on television screens outside the church, the update said.

Before that, her coffin will be brought to her Mumbles home on 15 August at 15:30 BST, with people invited to "pay their respects" by lining Newton Road at 15:15.

Tyler, born Gaynor Hopkins - also known for hits including Holding Out For A Hero and Here She Comes - is survived by her husband of more than 50 years, Robert Sullivan.

Oscar-winning actress and Wednesday star Catherine Zeta-Jones led the tributes to Bonnie Tyler earlier this month, saying her heart was "broken" with the news that "our dearest Bonnie Tyler has passed away".

The Prince of Wales described Tyler as a "proud Welsh icon" and Sir Cliff Richard said he was mourning "another wonderful friend gone too soon".

In May, Tyler was placed into an induced coma after having emergency intestinal surgery in Portugal.

Last month, her spokesperson said she was out of the coma but remained "very unwell and in intensive care".

Total Eclipse of the Heart, her biggest hit, topped the charts on both sides of the Atlantic.

In doing so, she became the first Welsh person to score a number one hit in the US.

The Total Eclipse of the Heart singer died "unexpectedly" on 8 July, aged 75, at a Portugal hospital, "as a result of the illness that she was being treated for", her family said. The...

Entertainment.title: Molly Smith and Tom Clare: Love Island winners marry
Entertainment.text:
Love Island: All Stars winners Molly Smith and Tom Clare have tied the knot.

The couple, who won the first series of the spin-off edition in 2024, announced on Instagram, external they were "just married" alongside a photo of them kissing while flower petals floated around them.

Molly, 32, wore a white short-sleeved mini dress with a matching pillbox hat and netted veil for the occassion, while Tom, 26, wore a black suit and tie.

They announced their engagement in September last year after Tom proposed in Dubai in front of the luxury hotel, Burj Al Arab, with a host of candles and flowers.

Sharing more from their marriage ceremony, Molly posted a photo to her Instagram story of her hand on top of Tom's.

The snap showed their wedding rings along with a bouquet of flowers, with the caption: "Husband and wife".

The couple first got together two years ago, when they met on season one of Love Island: All Stars.

All Stars is a spin-off of the hit dating show, which sees past contestants return for a second chance at finding love in the villa.

During the reality dating show, the pair beat Molly's ex-boyfriend Callum Jones and his partner at the time, Jessica Gale, who finished as runners-up.

Molly previously starred in series six in 2020 and left the show with Callum, who she was in a relationship with for three years before they split.

While Hyde United footballer Tom previously came third place on the second winter edition of Love Island in 2023 with former partner Samie Elishi.

Earlier this year, Tom and Molly took over hosting duties of the NearlyWeds podcast from radio presenters and reality stars Jamie Laing and Sophie Habboo.

Fellow Love Island stars were among those to congratulate the couple, including former winner Casey O'Gorman who described it as the "best day".

Sophie Piper, who came third on their All Star season, and TikTok star GK Barry also shared their support with the newly weds.

Molly, 32, wore a white...

Entertainment.title: The Odyssey film fans inspired to go back to the original Homer poem
Entertainment.text:
Sir Christopher Nolan's big-screen version of The Odyssey has become one of the box office hits of the year - and has also sparked interest in the original Greek myth, with book sales and audiobook listening figures rising sharply.

The director's take on Homer's epic poem took $264m (£198m) at cinema box offices globally in its opening weekend, including $52m (£39m) on giant Imax screens.

Some of those fans have been inspired to go back to the source, with sales of print editions rising by 700% in the UK last week compared with the same period in 2025.

An audio version read by Sir Stephen Fry is now in the Spotify UK audiobook top 10, while Sir Ian McKellen's narration has entered the equivalent chart in the US.

Almost 8,000 print copies of The Odyssey were sold in the UK last week, taking the total so far this year to 47,000. That's a 95% year-on-year increase, according to NielsenIQ BookData.

British chain Waterstones said there had been "phenomenal" demand, particularly for the 2017 translation by Emily Wilson, which Sir Christopher has cited as one of his sources.

"We got more stock in but we weren't anticipating quite the uplift, particularly on Emily Wilson," Waterstones chief operating officer Kate Skipper told BBC Radio 4's Today programme.

"So we're getting deliveries every day and they're flying out as soon as they land in the shops."

Wilson herself welcomed the impact of the film, but pointed out that Sir Christopher has left out some crucial elements.

"I want to celebrate the fact that so many more people are aware of The Odyssey and were brought back to the poem," she told the Associated Press, external.

"But at the same time, I had the sense that Christopher Nolan was trying to pack everything in while missing themes that were essential to the poem."

Many of those who have gone back to the ancient story are younger people who are listening to the audiobook version.

Listening figures on Spotify have risen by about 680% since the film's release on Friday, Variety reported, external - with more than 80% of the listeners being Gen Z or millennials.

In cinemas, the film's global takings include £12.6m from the UK - making it the second-biggest opening weekend in the country so far this year, behind Toy Story 5.

In the process, The Odyssey broke the record for the highest takings in an opening weekend for a film on Imax screens in the UK, according to industry publication Screen Daily, external.

The three-hour epic, which stars Matt Damon, Tom Holland and Anne Hathaway, was the first theatrical release to be shot entirely with Imax 70mm film cameras.

While there are more than 50 Imax screens in the UK, only three are showing it using 70mm film projectors - the BFI and Science Museum in London, and Printworks in Manchester.

"You do feel like you're very close to the characters, you're immersed in the action. So it is a heightened experience, I would say," film critic and Girls on Film podcast host Anna Smith told the Today programme.

Imax screenings are more expensive than regular cinema tickets, however.

"If you have the money and you've been lucky enough to book, I don't think you would regret seeing it in Imax 70mm," Metro film critic Tori Brazier said.

"But at the same time it's very expensive and we do live in a world where lots of people watch films on laptop screens and phone screens now, so if you're going to the cinema you're going to have a big-screen experience regardless."

The director's take on Homer's epic poem took $264m (£198m) at cinema box offices...

Entertainment.title: Oasis overtake The Beatles in all-time UK album chart
Entertainment.text:
Oasis's second album (What's The Story) Morning Glory? has officially overtaken the biggest-selling album by their heroes The Beatles in the list of the UK's most popular albums of all-time, external.

The Gallagher brothers' 1995 album, which contains hits like Wonderwall and Don't Look Back in Anger, is third on the all-time best-sellers list, which has been published by the Official Charts Company.

The Beatles' 1967 psychedelic masterpiece Sgt Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band, which was third on the same list a decade ago, external, has now dropped to number five.

Queen's 1981 Greatest Hits collection remains the biggest seller overall with 1992's Abba Gold in second place, while Adele's 2011 album 21 has moved up to fourth place.

The all-time list was released on Wednesday to mark the 70th anniversary of the weekly UK album chart.

In total, (What's The Story) Morning Glory? has racked up 6.2 million UK chart units - streams and sales combined - according to the Official Charts Company.

Last year, there was renewed interest in Oasis's music due to their much publicised worldwide reunion tour. The setlist lent heavily on tracks from the album, including Some Might Say and Champagne Supernova.

In 2022, Queen's compilation LP made history as the first album to pass seven million UK chart units.

Only Swedish pop maestros Abba have breached the seven million mark since, making it an elite club.

Meanwhile, Fleetwood Mac's 1977 classic Rumours and Bob Marley's best-of album Legend have both moved into the all-time top 10 over the past 10 years.

UK Official Albums Chart - all-time top 10

Greatest Hits - Queen

(What's the Story) Morning Glory - Oasis

Sgt Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band - The Beatles

Rumours - Fleetwood Mac

The Dark Side of the Moon - Pink Floyd

Thriller - Michael Jackson

Legend - Bob Marley and The Wailers

Brothers in Arms - Dire Straits

(What's the Story) Morning Glory? received surprisingly mixed reviews from the British music press when it was released in October 1995. But it went on to win best British album at the 1996 Brit Awards and has stood the test of time.

Wonderwall, from the album, became hugely popular again this summer with England fans and players singing it together at the World Cup in North America.

The booming rock ballad Don't Look Back in Anger also took on a greater significance after it was sung, impromptu, at a vigil for those who lost their lives in the 2017 Manchester Arena attack.

Becca Monahan and Chris Austin, interim co-managing directors of the Official Charts, congratulated Oasis on the album's achievements in a joint statement.

"Three decades on, its scale and influence remain extraordinary - a defining British record that continues to unite generations, fill stadiums and soundtrack lives," they noted.

Oasis Songwriter Noel Gallagher was born three days after The Beatles released their Sgt Pepper album.

The Gallagher brothers' 1995 album, which contains hits like Wonderwall and Don't Look Back in Anger, is third on...

Entertainment.title: Kaylee Hottle: 'Devastated' Millie Bobby Brown leads tributes
Entertainment.text:
Millie Bobby Brown is among the stars who have remembered the "talent" and "kindness" of US actress Kaylee Hottle, who died in a car crash aged 18.

Hottle was killed in a single-vehicle accident in Frederick, Maryland on Tuesday morning.

The young actress, who was deaf, appeared alongside Bobby Brown in the 2021 blockbuster Godzilla vs Kong.

Bobby Brown shared her sadness, alongside a black and white image of Hottle, to her Instagram story.

Over the photo, the Stranger Things star wrote: "I'm so devastated to hear this.

"You will be deeply missed Kaylee."

Hottle played a young orphan named Jia in the monster film series, and reprised the role in the 2024 sequel Godzilla x Kong: The New Empire.

British actress Rebecca Hall, who featured in both films with Hottle, paid tribute by sharing pictures of them together on set.

"My heart goes out to your family. You will be missed Kaylee," Hall said.

Fellow co-star and Mexican actress Eiza González said her "heart is absolutely broken" as she posted a photo of them posing in costume.

"I'm devastated. I adore you," wrote González.

"I know heaven has the sweetest kindest girl. We will miss you terribly."

Legendary Pictures, the production company behind the Godzilla movies, described Hottle as a "cherished member" of the production.

"Through her unforgettable portrayal of Jia, Kaylee brought extraordinary heart, humanity, and light to Godzilla, Kong, and the Monsterverse," the statement on Instagram said., external

"Her kindness, warmth, and remarkable talent touched everyone fortunate enough to work alongside her."

Oscar winning deaf actress Marlee Matlin also shared a photo to her Instagram, external of herself and fellow actress Shoshannah Stern alongside Hottle.

Matlin said she was "absolutely gutted" about the passing of "sweet Kaylee Hottle."

"May her beauty and talent be a memory for eternity."

Over the years, Hottle had worked to advocate for the deaf community.

Teri Devine, associate director of inclusion at UK charity Royal National Institute for Deaf People (RNID), told BBC Newsbeat that Hottle helped "pave the way" for greater representation.

She described her as a "passionate advocate for the deaf community and deaf actors in Hollywood" and said she used her platform to "raise awareness and inspire others to drive positive change for people who are deaf or have hearing loss".

"Representation of people who are deaf or have hearing loss in the spotlight is incredibly important," Devine added.

"Kaylee helped to improve understanding, challenge misconceptions, and prove there are no limits to what people who are deaf or have hearing loss can achieve."

The actress also set an example to young people, a spokesperson for the National Deaf Children's Society said.

Martin McLean, the charity's deputy director of policy and campaigns, said Hottle "inspired deaf children and young people by showing that there are no limits to what they can achieve".

"Positive deaf role models help deaf young people feel less alone, build confidence in their identity and ambitions, and show them that anything is possible," he added.
Entertainment.description: Millie Bobby Brown is among the stars who have remembered the "talent" and "kindness" of US actress Kaylee Hottle, who died in a car crash aged 18. Hottle was killed in a single-vehicle accident in Frederick, Maryland on Tuesday morning. The young actress, who was deaf, appeared alongside Bobby Brown in the 2021...

Entertainment.title: ​’Ian Curtis didn’t have a chance’: the Durutti Column on departed friends, Factory Records and fun with Morrissey
Entertainment.text:
Vini, you’ve created some of the most beautiful, ethereal music of the last 40 years. Is it true that in your youth you were in a violent Manchester street gang? Songy24

Vini Reilly [guitars, vocals, keyboards]: Gangs then had a different meaning. It was just “their lot” and “our lot”. We’d sling insults at each other. Then you’d play football in the park and realise you were playing with the local thugs. They were actually very funny, and I could have fun with them because I was so little. At the time I’d been going through a recurring depressive illness and felt I didn’t fit in at home, so I left without any clean clothing. I wanted to end my life so I decided to go to Moss Side, where the gangsters would shoot me. Of course they didn’t. I’d had a very happy childhood until my father died when I was doing my O-levels, but I’ve come to the conclusion that the really bad things gave me more than they could ever take. How can you know happiness until you’ve known deep sadness?

Bruce Mitchell [drums]: Vini does kick off, though, properly battles. We had to section him once, and six people had to hold him down.

Legend has it that Joy Division helped cut and glue the sandpaper sheets on to the famous original cover of The Return of the Durutti Column. Were you involved? Gomgom71

VR: I walked into Factory Records to find Joy Division gluing them all. It was mortifying, so I escaped as quickly as I could.

BM: Vini denies this, but Hooky [Peter Hook] always says that Vini quickly stopped gluing them so he didn’t damage his nails. Joy Division had no cash, so they were paid to do it, which they did while watching porno movies. Alan Erasmus at Factory Records had got a load of thrown-away sleeves from an album of steam train noises: you can tell it’s an original edition because if you peel the sandpaper back you’ll find a steam train.

Is there a story behind your Blood Orange collaboration, The Field? nobblehobble

Keir Stewart [producer]: Dev Hynes [Blood Orange] has been a fan for a long time, and he sampled [the 1998 track] Sing to Me and made a tune out of it. He asked permission for the sample, and when we met we had this two-hour babble about the Durutti Column and all sorts. Durutti have all these famous covert fans: Stewart Lee, Chris Morris, Max Richter, Richard Ayoade, John Frusciante from the Red Hot Chili Peppers, Chris Martin from Coldplay, AG Cook, Caroline Polachek, loads of artists on the hyperpop scene. It’s great that Vini’s been able to harvest this recognition in his lifetime, but he keeps it all outside his door. He wants to remain unpolluted.

VR: I’ve never heard of most of these people, but it’s very nice.

Which guitarists did you listen to growing up? Troykatoo

VR: One of my favourite guitarists was Al Casey, who played with Thomas “Fats” Waller: really sparse melodies and harmonic progressions. I had this notion that whether you went to a big orchestral performance at the Free Trade Hall or a folk club in Sale, you knew exactly what the audience would look like, because people identify with genre. So I wanted to create something that wasn’t a recognisable form. When we were trying to get into Canada, the customs guy asked what kind of music we played and I piped up: “Avant garde jazz classical.” It was a joke, but it stuck.

How do you feel about the recent For Vini – A Tribute to the Durutti Column show in London? NickJMorgan

KS: Harry Styles told Radio 1 that the biggest influences on his new album are LCD Soundsystem and the Durutti Column. Then he was asked to curate the Meltdown festival, so we were asked if there was any way Vini could do a show. There wasn’t, so we got the musicians together for the tribute. I played guitar – I can’t play like Vini but put in a lot of practice! When Bruce came on to play drums, everyone was cheering. A promoter’s already asked if we could do a UK tour, so we’ll see.

VR: It’s fantastic that they’re doing it, but I couldn’t bear to see it. If Bruce is involved, it’s always right. Whenever we played together we didn’t rehearse, but we had such chemistry he always knew when a song would end. It worked every time.

Could you tell us your memories of Ian Curtis and the inspiration behind the song The Missing Boy? Philshawknight

VR: Ian and I were getting to know each other – we both worked for different parts of the civil service. We were planning to do some music together but never told anyone in case people like Mark E Smith called it nepotism. Then I was staying with Tony [Wilson, Factory boss] and Lindsay [Reade, Wilson’s then wife]. I answered the phone and it was Ian. He wasn’t making any sense. He fluctuated from being really positive to the opposite. He was angry that Tony had called him a poet. When I saw all the pills Ian had been prescribed for epilepsy, I knew he didn’t have a chance. Also his marriage was falling apart, he had a little girl … I wasn’t surprised when he killed himself: it’s all there in the songs, but nobody – not even Tony – ever asked him about them. There’s an anger to The Missing Boy because it’s like I’m gritting my teeth.

BM: I was living round the corner. Vini said he needed someone to go in the studio with him to record a memorial for someone who had died. He played it for the engineer, then played once more, leaning over to play the grand piano while his guitar went through a space echo. That second take is on the record.

Who is Paul in Detail for Paul? Uncleheinrichagain

VR: A friend I had for a long time who’d been at art school. Really intelligent, good guy. Then he got a job in advertising which went against his socialist principles. One day he just didn’t show up. I miss him. People always assume my songs are named after girlfriends, which they’re not at all. Jacqueline is Bruce’s wife, a fantastic artist. Quite a few are named after my nieces or cousins. Do they feel honoured? Oh no. My family are terribly insulted.

What was the best and worst thing about Tony Wilson? dramaagh

VR: There wasn’t a worst. The best is that he was a true altruist, and that never changed. Factory was conceived with the money his mother bequeathed to him after her death, and he was determined he was gonna do something out of respect for her. He made loads of recordings possible. A lot are very precious to me, because without him people would have never got to hear them.

Without Mercy is absolutely beautiful – a modern classical classic. How do you rate it among your work? FarmyardManure

VR: It was a nightmare to make, but I don’t regret it. Tony said: “Will you do an album for me?” How could I say no? He’d decided that Keats’s poem Without Mercy [La Belle Dame sans Merci] was the same formula as a pop song – boy meets girl, girl dumps boy – and wanted a musical interpretation. I don’t really like it [laughs], but I’m glad that Tony did, because it was a way of trying to thank him.

Was the name the Durutti Column deliberately or accidentally misspelt from the Spanish civil war anarchist Buenaventura Durruti and La Colonna Durruti? somna123

VR: The name was Tony’s idea. He felt that since anarchy was the basis of punk, we should go back to that idea. But he decided not to explain that to people, which was great for me because it meant I could waffle on talking absolute nonsense about it. It was probably misspelled by Factory as a mistake, but even in Spain some people think it looks better with one “r”.

What was it like to work on Viva Hate with Morrissey, and would you work with him again? deFaoiteK

VR: Certainly, because he’s so ridiculously gifted. I remember him talking about the Moors murderers and how he could have been one of those [murdered] kids, which never left him. On the song Late Night, Maudlin Street you really felt that doomy power. But also we never stopped laughing.

BM: I dropped Vini off at the studio half-expecting to pick him up again after half an hour, because all the musicians and engineers were eating at this long table, and right at the end on his own was Morrissey. But they got on so well that when I eventually picked him up, Vini said: “Can we take Mozza?” They were nattering in the back seat all the way. It was like listening to two old ladies on Coronation Street.

Songy24 Vini Reilly [guitars, vocals, keyboards]: Gangs then had a different meaning. It was just “their lot” and “our lot”. We’d sling insults at each...
Entertainment.author: SkunkNet Wire - theguardian.com

Entertainment.title: The roaring 20s: how the current decade revolutionised cinema
Entertainment.text:
Cinema has always been a medium of research and development. At any given point, the industry is searching for yet another breakthrough that can drive it into the future. Synchronised sound in the 1920s. Technicolor in the 1930s. The invention of the modern blockbuster in the 1970s.

Conversely, the consensus is that, if the 2020s has a defining trend, then it is one of decline. The theatrical experience is under attack from all angles: streaming, YouTube, a once-in-a-century health crisis that shut cinemas down entirely. And yet, if you look closer, this has actually been one of the most innovative decades that cinema has ever seen. Whether down to technology or desperation, the film industry has spent these last few years figuring out new ways to make films that simply could not have existed even at the turn of the decade.

Currently, the most notable of these is the way that cinema has become a conversation with YouTube. Back in 2005, when Jawed Karim uploaded the first video (the 19-second Me at the Zoo) to the platform, YouTube felt like a novelty. It was cheap and cheerful, a collection of cat videos and people falling over. How things have changed. Not only has it become the largest streaming platform in the world, but the first generation of adults who grew up with YouTube has come of age.

As we speak, the seventh and eighth biggest films of 2026 have been directed by people who learned their trade making content for YouTube. Obsession (worldwide gross: $442m) was directed by Curry Barker, a 26-year-old who dropped out of film school to make YouTube videos and TikToks. Meanwhile, Backrooms (worldwide gross: $384m) is even more explicitly internet-inspired. Directed by the then 20-year-old Kane Parsons, the film is based on a series of shorts he began uploading to YouTube in 2022, which in turn were inspired by some creepypasta (web-based horror content) that has been floating around online since 2019.

A decade ago, studios would have got this disastrously wrong. Ask them to adapt a YouTube video and they would have hired Michael Bay and Rihanna to make David After Dentist: The Motion Picture; it would have baffled anyone who didn’t watch YouTube – and appalled anyone who did. This is the first time that the industry has trusted natives to make the films they want to make. Clearly, given the enormous financial returns, it will not be the last. Steven Spielberg is now producing Alex Kister’s The Mandela Catalogue, based on a terrifying analogue horror series that has run on YouTube since 2021.

This would also be a good time to mention the rise of the copyright expiry movie. In 1998, the Copyright Term Extension Act was passed in the US, which extended protection to works to 95 years after they were first published. (In the UK, the rules are different; copyright extends to 70 years after the creator’s death.) This effectively meant that two decades went by without anything going out of copyright. When things finally caught up in 2019, freeing up anything made in 1923, there was a goldrush.

Now, let it be said that it wasn’t a particularly good goldrush. First out of the blocks was 2023’s Winnie-the-Pooh: Blood and Honey, a low-rent slasher movie whose only original thought was to have Winnie-the-Pooh in it. Then came The Mouse Trap and Screamboat (both based on Steamboat Willie, the first element of the Mickey Mouse character to have lost copyright protection), along with Popeye the Slayer Man and Bambi: The Reckoning. Without exception, these films are cheap, exploitative and burn out their premise long before the end credits – but more are to come.

Next year, if you can wait that long, will see the release of something called Poohniverse: Monsters Assemble, which will star – deep breath – Winnie-the-Pooh, Tigger, Bambi, Peter Pan, Tinker Bell, Pinocchio, Sleeping Beauty and the Cheshire Cat. It is also worth pointing out that King Kong enters the public domain in 2029, which will be brilliant news for anyone who has been longing to see a low-rent horror movie starring an unusually large gorilla.

Now, here is where I may lose you, because I am about to name Sonic the Hedgehog as the most important movie of the decade. Please bear with me. One of the most pervasive trends of this decade has been the way that, for good or ill, audiences have hijacked movies to their own ends. The biggest example is obviously Barbenheimer, in which Barbie and Oppenheimer were released on the same day, kickstarting a phenomenon where people bought tickets to see one straight after the other. Nobody in the film industry expected this, but the publicity from this grassroots celebration drove receipts of both films through the roof.

The flipside to this is Morbius, a terrible film that inspired no end of ironic online mockery – but what was hilarious was that Sony mistook the irony for sincere support, and rereleased the film on 1,000 screens to cash in. It goes without saying that nobody watched it. Also, think of all the extra teenagers who bought tickets for Minions: The Rise of Gru, purely so they could watch dressed in suits and babble nonsense to participate in the Gentleminions trend, or the people who went to see A Minecraft Movie exclusively to scream and chuck stuff about when Jack Black said the words “chicken jockey”.

No film, arguably, has benefited from fan reaction quite like Sonic the Hedgehog. The first we saw of it was a nightmarish trailer featuring an unrecognisable main character. Sonic had bizarrely human proportions, as well as tiny eyes and teeth that looked as if they had been stolen from someone’s mouth while they slept. It was awful, and the internet wasted no time in saying that it was awful. In times gone by, studios would have simply taken the hit and released the film as quietly as possible, writing it off as another godawful video game adaptation destined to die on impact. But this time, producers listened. The trailer was released on Tuesday. By Thursday, the director had tweeted that the character would be completely redesigned. Six months after that, New Sonic was unveiled, and fans were delighted.

With a character that people could actually stand to look at, Sonic the Hedgehog went on to become the sixth biggest film of 2020. Nobody saw this coming. Until Sonic, video game adaptations were universally written off as the lowest form of cinema. Think of the dismal live action Super Mario Bros movie, or Mark Wahlberg’s baffling Max Payne, or Uwe Boll’s irredeemable Postal. About 46 English language video game adaptations were released between 1993 and 2019, and they made a combined total of $5.4bn. But A Minecraft Movie made close to a $1bn on its own. The animated Super Mario Movie released in 2023 ended the year second only to Barbie. At time of writing, The Super Mario Galaxy Movie is the biggest film of 2026 so far worldwide. By demonstrating to other film-makers how to give people what they actually want to see, Sonic has turned the video game adaptation into a licence to print money.
Entertainment.description: Cinema has always been a medium of research and development. At any given point, the industry is searching for yet another breakthrough that can drive it into the future. Synchronised sound in the 1920s. Technicolor in the 1930s. The invention of the modern blockbuster in the 1970s. Conversely, the consensus is...
Entertainment.author: SkunkNet Wire - theguardian.com

BreakingNewsTab.title: US imposes tariffs on dozens of trade partners over 'forced labour' imports
BreakingNewsTab.text:
The US is imposing new tariffs on around 60 trading partners accounting for the vast majority of its imports over claims they failed to properly stop forced labour.

The duties, ranging from 10% to 12.5%, target key economic partners – including the UK, China, EU, Canada, Japan and India. They come in on Friday, as a temporary 10% tax on foreign goods introduced earlier this year expires.

The move is the latest escalation in the global trade war reignited by US President Donald Trump when he returned to office last year.

The US Supreme Court ruled earlier this year that many of the tariffs imposed globally under emergency powers were illegally enacted.

So the president has since sought other legal avenues to pursue his flagship trade policy.

It was last month that the White House first proposed a series of 10-12.5% duties on goods arriving to American shores from dozens of countries over concerns they were not doing enough to tackle forced labour.

On Thursday, US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer, acting under Trump's direction, said those duties would now take effect.

"Today's action will begin to correct what is both a human rights abuse and distortive trade practice to improve the welfare of workers everywhere," his statement said.

Greer invoked Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974, which governs US trade enforcement of practices that burden or restrict American commerce.

Earlier this week the Trump administration invoked a different statute, Section 338 of the Tariff Act of 1930, to impose 50% tariffs on products from Canada.

On Thursday, the Office of the US Trade Representative said the latest tariffs were being imposed on partners "for their failure to impose and effectively enforce a prohibition on the importation of goods produced with forced labour".

The new duties apply to the top 60 US trade partners covering 99.4% of US imports, it added.

The office said Trump in his second term had made adoption of a ban on imports made with forced labour a "critical" part of reciprocal trade agreements with other nations.

It said so far 10 trading partners had agreed to enact such a ban in these agreements, and other countries had implemented bans in response to its investigations in recent weeks.

Trading partners that have "made commitments to adopt, and effectively enforce" bans on forced labour imports will be subject to a 10% tariff, while those that have not will have the higher 12.5% rate, the office added.

Greer said in his statement he was "encouraged by the trading partners who have moved quickly to adopt forced labour import prohibitions, and look forward to ensuring their effective enforcement".

The new levies show the Trump administration is "determined" to push on with its tariff strategy, said trade policy expert Deborah Elms from the Hinrich Foundation.

It is unlikely countries hit with tariffs will be able to prove that they have sufficient measures to prevent forced labour imports, she told the BBC.

The levies are likely to raise costs for businesses and consumers, although its impact could be softened due to the number of exempted goods, said the Asia Society Policy Institute's economic security expert Wendy Cutler.

Most trading partners will be disappointed with the new levies and are likely to focus on ways to "reduce their dependence on the US market" by making deals with other countries, Cutler said.
BreakingNewsTab.description: The US is imposing new tariffs on around 60 trading partners accounting for the vast majority of its imports over claims they failed to properly stop forced labour. The duties, ranging from 10% to 12.5%, target key economic partners – including the UK, China, EU, Canada, Japan and India. They come in on Friday, as...

BreakingNewsTab.title: Burnham pauses early prison release scheme
BreakingNewsTab.text:
Andy Burnham has announced he is pausing a scheme which would have seen thousands of prisoners in England and Wales eligible to be released early.

It came after criticism of the scheme from survivors and victims' families, including the widow of PC Andrew Harper who described the possibility of his killers' early release as "deplorable".

The Sentencing Act, which was due to come into effect in September, would have seen some offenders let out at varying stages in a bid to ease prison overcrowding.

The new Justice Secretary Alex Norris said the pause aimed to "give victims and survivors certainty" and the government wanted to make sure it got "the implementation of this policy right".

He added a review would also look at the information given to survivors and victims' families as part of the early release process.

Burnham earlier said: "No prisoners will be let out under this policy until we have conducted an urgent review and done everything we can to minimise risk to the public."

He added: "Where changes are needed, we will not hesitate to make them."

Under the act, some offenders would have been let out after serving a third of their sentence, rather than half.

The rest of their sentence would be spent in the community, where they would be monitored and could be returned to prison if they offended again.

More serious offenders would have been released after serving half of their sentence, rather than two-thirds.

The policy was brought in to ease overcrowding in the prison system but has been criticised by opposition parties, as well as victims and their families.

The scheme would have seen around 700 prisoners released in early September, the BBC understands.

The decision to pause the scheme came after criticism from the family of Thames Valley Police officer Andrew Harper, who was killed in Berkshire in 2019 when he was dragged by a car down a country road by three teenagers.

Two of the killers of PC Harper, who were both sentenced to 13 years in prison, would have benefited from the scheme from June 2027, when the longest fixed sentences would have been added to the scheme.

PC Harper's widow, Lissie, called the plans "deplorable" and said politicians were "making decisions from behind a desk that will have real and lasting consequences" for the families of victims.

His mother, meanwhile, said her son had not been given "proper justice" and "every victim will be feeling the same" about the early prison release scheme.

Thousands of letters had been sent to victims in recent weeks, warning them that the early release scheme was coming in September.

Norris, who voted in support of the Sentencing Act under Starmer's government, later said the decision to pause was made after "listening to victims" and he hoped to provide them "certainty as soon as we can".

He said: "The prime minister and I have heard from victims and survivors and from people across the country about their concerns about the implementation of some very important elements of the Sentencing Act."

One element the government needed to get right was the information provided to victims before prisoners were released, Norris added.

Norris refused to be drawn on why he did not raise concerns when the Sentencing Act was first introduced but said Burnham was "quite right" to review the scheme.

Jade Belgrove, a survivor of sexual assault who has been campaigning against the early release scheme, said it was "such a relief that we've been heard".

She told BBC News that when she received a letter informing her that her attacker might be released early "it was horrific".

Belgrove said it even led her to question whether reporting the assault in the first place was worth it.

It came after criticism of the scheme from survivors and victims' families, including the widow of PC Andrew Harper who described the possibility of his killers' early...

BreakingNewsTab.title: West Mersea beach tragedy victims were trying to help boy
BreakingNewsTab.text:
A woman and her 15-year-old daughter who died after getting into difficulty in the water on the Essex coast were trying to help a seven-year-old boy, police have said.

The child remains in a critical condition in hospital after the incident at West Mersea, near Colchester, on Wednesday evening. Three others were taken to hospital.

The victims have been named by a family member as Shally Rahman, 41, and teenager Samiha Rahman. Police said all the people involved knew each other.

Shally's brother-in-law Matiur Rahman said the family was enjoying a seaside holiday when a "big wave came and took them".

"My whole family, we are, like, speechless," said Matiur Rahman, who used to live in Bradford, but spoke to the BBC from Bangladesh.

"We are very shocked. We can't explain [what has happened]."

He said his niece was a "very, very good person" and his sister-in-law was "a very helpful person".

Essex Police - which was called by the ambulance service at 17:35 BST to the water near Seaview Avenue - said the victims were from London.

"At this stage we believe they were trying to help a seven-year-old boy who had got into difficulties," said Det Ch Insp Al Pitcher.

He said officers had been speaking to "dozens" of witnesses to establish the circumstances leading up to the tragedy, and asked anyone else with information to contact the force.

The detective thanked first responders who worked "tirelessly" to try saving their lives.

The East of England Ambulance Service said the deceased could not be resuscitated "despite best efforts". A spokesperson said four more people in total were taken to hospital, including one by air ambulance.

Carl Powling, a Conservative Colchester city councillor for West Mersea, said the HM Coastguard and RNLI teams were "very quick off the mark" and "very professional".

His 19-year-old daughter Leonie Powling, who works at the cafe, was one of the teenagers who assisted at the scene.

She tried looking for a paddleboard she could use to get to the family in the water, and described watching people trying to rescue them and then administer CPR.

"We did what we could," said Leonie Powling.

High tide was expected at 18:47 that evening and Carl Powling said it could frequently "drag you out to sea".

"If you are not familiar with the area, it can be very dangerous," he said.

Claire Douglas, whose house backs on to the beach, told the BBC that "nobody" left the scene on Wednesday until "they'd done as much as they could".

"It's just so devastating," said a tearful Douglas.

"You just get caught up in the sort of panic thinking - 'there's nothing I can physically do' - you're just standing watching."

Additional reporting by Lewis Adams, Zoe Applegate and Tom Larsen-Wright.

The child remains in a critical condition in hospital after the incident at West Mersea, near Colchester, on Wednesday evening. Three others were...

BreakingNewsTab.title: UK complacent about war threat, warns BAE Systems' boss
BreakingNewsTab.text:
The UK has been too complacent about the risk of foreign attack, the boss of Europe's biggest defence contractor BAE Systems has warned.

Dr Charles Woodburn told the BBC in an exclusive interview that the level of threat was the highest he'd seen in his lifetime.

He welcomed the recent boost to defence spending but said much more would be needed to hit the government's commitment to spend 3.5% of GDP on defence by 2035.

He also warned we were not far from the use of autonomous lethal weapons by countries that may not follow the UK's policy of maintaining human control.

In a rare interview, Woodburn told the BBC the reality of modern warfare had "changed quite profoundly" and that UK defence spending had to change with it.

He said Russia and Ukraine had become "incredibly adept" at using autonomy in the form of drones and counter drones.

"It's something that we now have to really understand and make sure that we're able to provide the capabilities that can counter that and deter aggression," he said.

BAE Systems unveiled a life-size model of an unmanned fighter jet in front of the new Defence Secretary Wes Streeting at the Farnborough International Air Show.

Four of these so-called Collaborative Combat Aircraft will accompany a manned fighter to massively increase firepower but at just 20% of the cost of a piloted aircraft.

However, Woodburn said there was still a role for the traditional battleships and submarines that BAE has been manufacturing for decades.

"If you look at what our adversaries are building, they're also building significantly large platforms - aircraft, ships, submarines. And they're supplemented by some of these unmanned capabilities." It is the combination of the two that has the "winning" formula, he said.

It is hardly surprising that the boss of a defence company would like to see more defence spending. But Woodburn said the UK had been complacent for some time about the level of threat it faced - particularly from Russia.

"This is by far the most threatening time I've seen," he said. "Even when I joined the company in 2016 I was amazed at what we saw Russian submarines doing, the cyber attacks. I think that's come a long way sadly as a result of the Ukraine conflict we're much more aware of the proximity of a hot war in Europe."

The war readiness of the UK's armed forces has been questioned by senior armed services figures and openly mocked by the US general Sir Gwyn Jenkins, the First Sea Lord, recently said the navy is not ready for war.

Pete Hegseth, the US defence secretary sarcastically referred to the "big bad Royal Navy" while President Donald Trump described the UK's aircraft carriers as "toys".

That impression was not helped by the tardiness of mobilising the destroyer HMS Dragon – built by BAE - to provide air cover for Cyprus against Iranian and Hezbollah threats.

Woodburn insisted the company had responded as soon as the Ministry of Defence had asked for its help.

He said BAE gets "things in place as quickly as we're able to but at the end of the day you need a signal to move".

Woodburn welcomed the promotion of former Defence Secretary John Healey to chancellor. Healey very publicly resigned from the post insisting that the extra money awarded to defence "falls well short of what is needed to protect the country".

An additional £13.5bn was boosted to an extra £15bn after he resigned but that will only bring spending on defence to 2.68% of GDP by 2030 – well short of the 3% Healey said was required. A further massive increase in spending will be needed to hit the government goal of 3.5% by 2035.

Shares in defence companies – including BAE – rose on the news that Healey was moving into number 11 but Woodburn knows he will have other departments competing for cash.

"We have tremendous respect for John," he said. "He understands what is needed and I think in that sense its very good he's in the Treasury role."

He recognised Healey would have a host of complicated national finances to tackle. "But the fact that he understands the requirements of defence we see as a very positive thing", he said.

The UK's armed forces have dwindled since the end of the cold war. In 1990 – the army had 153,000 regular soldiers. Today that number is just 74,000.

But it is debatable how many human soldiers you need these days and Woodburn said a new era of autonomous lethal weaponry was close at hand.

"We're not far from that. While we follow rules of 'man in the loop' we know very well that our adversaries are not always doing that," he said.

At the air show, new UK Prime Minister Andy Burnham sent a video with a message that defence could play a big role in his plans to reindustrialise the economy and provide thousands of skilled jobs.

And in his keynote speech at the airshow Streeting said: "Defence will not just be a recipient of the proceeds of economic growth. It will be a central contributor to building that growth, a driver of prosperity in parts of the country that are crying out for the backing they need to make their contribution to the challenge of our time."

Woodburn said he wanted to help make the case for the knock-on benefits of defence spending.

"We need to make sure that the industrial benefits, the jobs benefits are well told so people understand this is money well spent," he said.

But does he ever lose sleep over the fact that his products are ultimately intended to kill people?

"The thing I always think about is that the best thing we can do is to deter. Detering war means our brave men and women have the best kit they could possibly have. Our mission as a business is to protect those who protect us and we take that incredibly seriously."
BreakingNewsTab.description: The UK has been too complacent about the risk of foreign attack, the boss of Europe's biggest defence contractor BAE Systems has warned. Dr Charles Woodburn told the BBC in an exclusive interview that the level of threat was the highest he'd seen in his lifetime. He welcomed the recent boost to defence spending but...

BreakingNewsTab.title: Commonwealth Games: King Charles III emerges from the Tardis as Glasgow 2026 Games begin
BreakingNewsTab.text:
King Charles III, the Loch Ness Monster and thousands of athletes and Para-athletes took centre stage as the 2026 Commonwealth Games officially began with the opening ceremony.

King Charles and Queen Camilla were greeted by cycling great Sir Chris Hoy as they arrived into Glasgow's Hydro Arena in Doctor Who's Tardis – a reference to the blue police boxes seen around the city.

After taking their seats alongside new Prime Minister Andy Burnham, who was attending his first major engagement since replacing Sir Keir Starmer, athletes from the 74 competing nations and territories entered the arena.

Those from Botswana were the first to emerge, with athletes dressed in the colours of their nations, including Ghana's red, green, yellow and black stripes, South Africa in Springbok-style rugby jerseys, and Barbados in blue and yellow combined with tartan scarves.

One of the louder cheers came for the Falklands Islands – in the news recently following Argentina's victory over England in the football World Cup semi-finals – and another followed when the Samoa team emerged and performed a haka in front of the dignitaries in the Royal Box.

But nothing compared to the noise which greeted the arrival of the Scotland team, who entered the arena last in their white shirts and kilts and lifted many in the capacity crowd from their seats.

A large Loch Ness Monster representation then emerged, before, backed by bagpipers and drummers, a rousing rendition of Flower of Scotland followed.

The next section of the evening featured theatrical performances – a show billed as "a love letter to Glasgow and the Commonwealth".

It began with a performance by Scottish folk and country singer Nathan Evans and a dance under a replica of Glasgow Central Station's iconic clock.

The saying "Meet me under the clock" is famous in these parts.

Singer Callum Beattie's rendition of Baccara's Yes Sir, I Can Boogie – the 1970s disco tune recently adopted as Scotland's unofficial anthem - had the Scottish athletes dancing in their seats.

Held indoors in front of about 10,000 people, this was a more intimate ceremony than the last time Glasgow hosted the Commonwealth Games, when a 40,000-strong crowd witnessed the opening of the Games in 2014 at Celtic Park.

There was a point, after the Australian state of Victoria withdrew as hosts in 2023, when some thought these Games would not happen. Glasgow stepped in at short notice to ensure otherwise.

There are eight fewer sports involved compared to 2014, given the short build-up, and venues from 12 years ago have been repurposed.

But Commonwealth Sport president Donald Rukare, wearing a tartan kilt in the colours of his home country Uganda, said this edition has been made possible by the "extraordinary legacy of 2014 and by a city that once again welcomes us with warm hearts and open arms".

George Black, chair of Glasgow 2026, added: "When the future of these Games was uncertain, Glasgow didn't ask: 'Can we?'

"We asked: 'When do we get started?'

"People across this city should be incredibly proud of what has been achieved over the past 18 months.

"Venues opened their doors. Communities opened their hearts.

"Businesses, artists, schools, volunteers, partners, neighbours and the people of Glasgow came together with one shared belief: that the Commonwealth Games matter and that the world deserves to experience them."

Scottish singer KT Tunstall's rendition of No Scotland No Party – another anthem of the Tartan Army this summer – later in the evening was fitting on this occasion.

Prior to that, the King's Baton was brought into the arena while singer Nina Nesbitt performed another Scottish classic, the Proclaimers' classic I'm Gonna Be (500 Miles).

The King used his speech to stress the importance of preventing plastic pollution in oceans and waterways.

"I extend my warmest gratitude to the city of Glasgow and all those have made this event possible," he said.

"As we share our love of sport and our planet, let us build a harmonious future for our families and each other."

The sporting action runs across the next 10 days before the Games conclude with the closing ceremony on Sunday, 2 August, back at the Hydro.

The first medals are set to be won in Para-powerlifting in the early evening of Friday.

King Charles and Queen Camilla were greeted by cycling great Sir Chris Hoy as they arrived into Glasgow's Hydro Arena in Doctor Who's...

BreakingNewsTab.title: UK 'ready to defend itself' after Iran says bases are 'legitimate targets'
BreakingNewsTab.text:
The British government has said it "stands ready 24/7 to defend itself" after Iran said UK bases used by the US were considered "legitimate targets".

The UK has allowed the US to launch "defensive" operations from British bases hosting American personnel and aircraft, while refusing to cooperate in offensive operations against Iran.

Iran's foreign ministry said the UK was an "accomplice" in the US war, while the powerful Islamic Revolution Guards Corps (IRGC) military wing said any country which supports the US militarily "will be responsible for the consequences and repercussions".

It came after the US carried out a 12th consecutive night of strikes against targets in Iran.

While US President Donald Trump has repeatedly criticised the British government and former Prime Minister Sir Keir Starmer for what he has deemed a lack of support for his war, Iran's comments on 23 July sought to paint the UK as a party to the campaign.

In March, days into the US-Israel led war, permission was given to the US for it to use British bases including RAF Fairford in Gloucestershire and Diego Garcia in the Indian Ocean to target Iranian military infrastructure used to attack Gulf countries and vessels in the Strait of Hormuz.

That policy has not been altered by new Prime Minister Andy Burnham, who was notified last week that a decision had been taken to extend the agreement with the US.

In a statement published on the IRGC-linked Tasnim News Agency on 23 July, the group said American bombing missions had been launched from RAF Fairford two days earlier.

The UK Ministry of Defence has not commented on whether the base was used by the US for operations against Iran this week.

Responding to the Iranian threats, a UK government spokesperson said: "Our Armed Forces are ready to keep the United Kingdom safe from any kind of attacks, whether it's on our soil or from abroad. The UK stands ready 24/7 to defend itself.

"This includes through operating a layered approach to air and missile defence, provided by Royal Navy, British Army and Royal Air Force assets equipped with a range of advanced capabilities, working closely with our Nato allies.

"The UK's approach to the conflict remains the same - we are committed to defending our people, our interests and our allies, acting in accordance with international law and not getting drawn into the wider conflict."

Days after the war began on 28 February, Iran's ambassador to London, Seyed Ali Mousavi, said Iran would have the "right to self-defence" if the UK joined the war, and said it should be "very careful" about taking part.

On 19 March, Iran fired two ballistic missiles at the UK's Diego Garcia base in the Chagos Islands. One reportedly failed mid-flight, with the other shot down by a US warship.

There were doubts then over whether Iran had missiles which were capable of reaching the archipelago, which is about 2,360 miles (3,600km) from Iran.

The same month, Israel claimed Iran possessed missiles that could reach up to 4,000km (2,485 miles), which would theoretically mean they could reach the UK and mainland European countries.

However, then Housing Secretary Steve Reed told BBC News at the time there was "no specific assessment that the Iranians are targeting the UK - or even could if they wanted to".

Analysts have also raised doubts over the reliability and accuracy of Iran's missile arsenal, which the US says has been severely degraded by its strikes.

Iran has launched strikes against several countries in the Gulf which house US military bases in recent months, while attacks against civilian vessels in the Strait of Hormuz have effectively closed the vital shipping route.

The UK has allowed the US to launch "defensive" operations from British bases hosting American personnel and aircraft, while refusing to cooperate in offensive...

BreakingNewsTab.title: Secrets of birdsongs' 'spectacular diversity' revealed
BreakingNewsTab.text:
Scientists in France say they have discovered the building blocks of the incredible diversity of birdsong.

A team analysed the songs of more than 100,000 songbirds and revealed that the world's birdsongs are composed of eight fundamental types of sound that the researchers call "motifs".

Birdsong all around the world, they say, is made up of combinations of these motifs - three types of trill, three types of whistle, chaotic notes and harmonies.

Birdsong is one of the natural world's most sophisticated forms of sound-based communication.

Its variety and complexity has fascinated biologists, but with so many different songs, it has been difficult to study on a global scale.

Lead researcher and University of Saint-Etienne PhD student Quentin Bacquelé explained: "The diversity is huge. And if you go to Asia, Africa, Amazonia, you will hear totally different songs."

In their new study, he and his colleagues used a global database of birdsong - recordings uploaded by thousands of volunteers. This meant they could analyse about 116,000 songs from more than 3,000 birdsong species.

They studied patterns in the songs - how each song shifted and changed through time. And with the help of AI, they could then compare the thousands of songs to each other - to find what connected them.

The scientists said this unveiled "a simplicity underlying the spectacular diversity of birdsongs".

A key finding was that the environment the birds evolved in - from tropical forest to temperate landscape - shaped how they "composed" their songs.

So birds adapted for tropical forests use different acoustic building blocks, or motifs, to those from temperate regions.

Simpler motifs, like flat whistles, Bacquelé explained, are often found in tropical environments, apparently because they help the birds make themselves heard through the busy, biodiverse soundscape and dense vegetation.

More complex compositions - with ultrafast trills and combinations that contain lots of information - are more common in temperate forests, where birds tend to communicate at closer range.

It's a kind of evolutionary trade-off: A very complex song provides a lot of information, but it is difficult to transmit that information over long distances in complex environments.
BreakingNewsTab.description: Scientists in France say they have discovered the building blocks of the incredible diversity of birdsong. A team analysed the songs of more than 100,000 songbirds and revealed that the world's birdsongs are composed of eight fundamental types of sound that the researchers call "motifs". Birdsong all around the...
BreakingNewsTab.author: SkunkNet Wire - bbc.co.uk

BreakingNewsTab.title: Southport killer Axel Rudakubana moved from Belmarsh prison to Broadmoor psychiatric hospital
BreakingNewsTab.text:
Axel Rudakubana, who murdered three children at a dance class in Southport, has been moved from prison to a secure psychiatric hospital.

The Ministry of Justice (MoJ) confirmed he was transferred from HMP Belmarsh to Broadmoor, the high-security psychiatric hospital in Berkshire.

Rudakubana murdered Elsie Dot Stancombe, seven, Bebe King, six, and Alice da Silva Aguiar, nine, and injured eight other children and two adults in an attack at a Taylor Swift-themed dance class in July 2024.

He was given a 52-year prison sentence in January 2025 but the judge was unable to impose a whole life order due to Rudakubana being 17 when he carried out the attack. He will likely never be released.

The then Attorney-General Lord Hermer said Rudakubana's sentence was the "second-longest sentence imposed by the courts in English history".

A spokesperson for the MoJ told PA: "The families of those murdered and injured in Southport will be in this country's heart forever and our thoughts remain with them.

"Any decision to transfer an offender is made following an independent, clinical assessment, under the Mental Health Act. Following rigorous security checks, the offender has been transferred to a high-security psychiatric unit, where he will remain until assessed fit to return to prison.

"Officials have approved this transfer, on behalf of Ministers. This transfer does not change his sentence – which remains at 52 years – and of which he will serve every single day."

Under the Mental Health Act, the MoJ is likely to have had no choice but to accept doctors' recommendations about whether Rudakubana could be held safely in prison.

The law requires he be transferred back to prison when he no longer requires treatment in hospital. His sentence is not being converted into a "hospital order", which would mean he stayed at Broadmoor.

The cost of keeping Rudakubana in hospital is likely to be much higher in Broadmoor than a prison.

According to the Centre for Mental Health the average secure hospital bed costs around £270,000 a year.

The average cost of keeping a prisoner in Belmarsh is £97,740 according to figures for 2023.

In May 2025 there was an investigation after Rudakubana, now 19, allegedly attacked a prison officer with boiling water.

The Southport Public Inquiry has heard evidence about his past mental health conditions.

A senior clinician at Alder Hey Children's Hospital described his "lack of empathy" combined with a "fascination with violence" and neurodiversity, which he said added up to a "really toxic mix".

According to West London NHS Trust, Broadmoor specialises in "providing assessment, treatment and care in conditions of high security" for men.

Is is the oldest of England and Wales's three high-security hospitals and has held some of the UK's most high-profile criminals.

It was renovated in 2017 and the BBC obtained pictures from inside the hospital. They show that older prison-like cells were replaced by more spacious rooms, though still with locks on the doors.
BreakingNewsTab.description: Axel Rudakubana, who murdered three children at a dance class in Southport, has been moved from prison to a secure psychiatric hospital. The Ministry of Justice (MoJ) confirmed he was transferred from HMP Belmarsh to Broadmoor, the high-security psychiatric hospital in Berkshire. Rudakubana murdered Elsie Dot...
BreakingNewsTab.author: SkunkNet Wire - bbc.co.uk

BreakingNewsTab.title: Oil prices hit $100 for the first time since May
BreakingNewsTab.text:
Oil prices hit $100 a barrel for the first time since May as the escalating conflict in the Middle East reignited fears over global energy supplies.

Brent crude - the global benchmark for oil prices - rose more than 6% on Thursday following several days of increases as the US stepped up military strikes against Iran.

Prices spiked after Houthi militia in Yemen attacked oil tankers in the Red Sea, threatening a key export route that Saudi Arabia has used to bypass the Strait of Hormuz.

Gas prices have also risen steadily over the past month, with the benchmark UK gas price currently at around 150 per therm, up from around 98p at the end of June.

Oil prices had been falling following a temporary ceasefire between the US and Iran.

They dropped back to levels last seen before the US and Israel began military action against Iran on 28 February.

However, the ceasefire has failed and this week US Secretary of State Marco Rubio said the people in charge in Iran were "not ready to make a deal".

The ongoing conflict risks pushing up inflation for many countries, including UK and the US leading to higher prices for consumers.

Higher oil prices typically lead to petrol and diesel becoming more expensive.

While drivers are affected directly, households could also see prices of other goods, such as food, increase due to businesses passing on higher transportation costs to customers.

Inflation has fallen both in the UK - down to 2.6% in the year to June helped by slowing diesel and petrol prices - and in the US to 3.5%.

But questions remain whether the slow down will prove short-lived due to the renewed conflict in the Middle East.

New data released on Thursday showed that UK petrol prices have risen by 5p a litre since the beginning of July, hitting reaching almost £1.56.

Diesel is at £1.72 a litre, on average, according to the RAC.

Average gasoline prices in the US have surpassed $4 a gallon once more, up from $3.92 a month ago, according to motorist advocacy group AAA.

"More expensive fuel and energy can ripple through the wider economy, increasing costs for businesses and ultimately feeding through into the price of food and other goods," said Jonathan Raymond, investment manager at Quilter Cheviot.

"This creates another headache for central banks as they continue their battle against inflation.

"If energy prices remain elevated, policymakers may come under pressure to keep interest rates higher for longer or even raise them. This would come as a blow to mortgage holders and borrowers already feeling the strain."
BreakingNewsTab.description: Oil prices hit $100 a barrel for the first time since May as the escalating conflict in the Middle East reignited fears over global energy supplies. Brent crude - the global benchmark for oil prices - rose more than 6% on Thursday following several days of increases as the US stepped up military strikes against...

BreakingNewsTab.title: Indonesia Singapore baby trafficking: What happens to the babies now?
BreakingNewsTab.text:
On Tuesday an Indonesian court convicted 19 people for baby trafficking, wrapping up a high-profile case - but leaving a key question unanswered.

The members of the trafficking syndicate were found guilty of procuring at least 34 babies and selling them for adoption.

Most of the children were bound for Singapore, and at least 12 babies were sent over.

The case has garnered attention in both countries for months. Now that it has ended, its verdict has sharpened the question of what will happen to the babies in Singapore.

Will they - and should they - return to Indonesia?

This is foremost in the minds of the adoptive parents in Singapore taking care of the babies. For months they have been on tenterhooks, waiting to find out whether they will get to keep their children.

Speaking to the BBC after the verdict, David, the Singaporean parent whom we interviewed previously, said he wanted both governments to make a decision soon.

"Since the case is concluded, how can we move on and what will happen to our son? It's been almost a year of fear and anxiety from waiting," said David, who spoke to us on condition we use a pseudonym for him.

"I sincerely hope that both authorities can come to a decision based on the best interests of the children.

"We respectfully ask that, in making this decision, consideration be given to the fact that both our son and our family are victims of the circumstances surrounding this case."

David and his wife Ally had previously told the BBC that they had no idea the child they adopted from Indonesia could have been trafficked. They said they had done their best to do their own background checks.

During the adoption process they had submitted to numerous checks from the Singaporean government which, they said, should have detected the trafficking.

Neither Indonesian nor Singaporean authorities gave definitive answers to the BBC's previous queries about what they intend to do with the babies.

Following the verdict, we asked them again.

Indonesia's foreign affairs ministry said "several aspects require further examination including the children's legal status, the identities of their biological parents, and the ongoing proceedings in Singapore".

It reiterated that it is "committed to prioritising the best interests of the children".

Singaporean authorities have yet to respond to our latest queries.

There is little data on what happens to children who have been trafficked within Indonesia after they are rescued, as the information is generally not made public, according to Ni Made Martini Puteri, a criminologist and lecturer at the University of Indonesia.

It is even less clear what happens to children who have been trafficked overseas.

Anti-trafficking and child rights groups in Indonesia have argued that the children in this case should return to their biological parents, as a matter of principle.

Speaking to BBC Indonesian after the verdict, Ai Maryanti, a commissioner with Indonesia's independent child protection commission, said that as the children's true identities have still not been established, their "best interests have not been given sufficient consideration".

Just because the children may already be adopted in Singapore, that "should not be treated as the final outcome", she added.

"Of course, children who are victims of human trafficking must be returned. Even if they're already adopted, what's the legal basis for that?

"What we don't want is Singapore saying that the adoptions complied with their law. According to our law, we found there was a high level of manipulation, resulting in Singapore easily accepting [the adoption applications]."

During the trial it emerged that the trafficking ring had submitted false information when applying for the infants' birth certificates and family cards.

They had listed the names of members of the trafficking ring as the children's birth mothers.

Local officials had issued the documents based on this information, raising questions about whether they failed to conduct adequate checks.

Indonesian authorities still have not identified all of the children's birth parents. This means that some children may have no one to return to.

If they are returned, they would be placed into state care. At least eight other babies rescued from the trafficking ring are already in the care of an orphanage in Bandung.

"Suppose a child is recognised as a trafficking victim and returned to Indonesia, but nobody here knows who the biological parents are because our evidentiary system is weak," said Puteri.

"I would argue that if the child has only known life in Singapore since infancy, then it makes more sense for the child to remain in Singapore with the adoptive parents.

"However, if the biological parents are later identified, the child should still have the right to know who they are."

With the verdict, Singapore should now treat these children as victims of trafficking and thus give them protection, she argued.

"Protection should also extend to the adoptive parents because they too may have had no idea what had happened," she added.

David, the Singaporean parent, said that he and his wife only wish to "live a normal family life, like any other couple hoping to raise a child in a safe, stable, and loving home.

"We hope that this matter can now be brought to a resolution so that our son can move forward with certainty and security."

Additional reporting by Farida Susanty of BBC Indonesian.
BreakingNewsTab.description: On Tuesday an Indonesian court convicted 19 people for baby trafficking, wrapping up a high-profile case - but leaving a key question unanswered. The members of the trafficking syndicate were found guilty of procuring at least 34 babies and selling them for adoption. Most of the children were bound for Singapore,...

BreakingNewsTab.title: Saudis must recognise Israel for nuclear deal, says Trump
BreakingNewsTab.text:
The deal between the US and Saudi Arabia to help the Gulf kingdom develop nuclear energy is dependent on Riyadh recognising the State of Israel, President Donald Trump has said.

"The Civil Nuclear Deal (There will be no enrichment of material!)... which pertains only to non-military use... will be approved, but is totally subject to Saudi Arabia joining the very respected and successful Abraham Accords," Trump said on Truth Social.

It was not immediately clear if the condition of joining the accords was stipulated in the text of the nuclear deal - which has not been released - or that Saudi Arabia had agreed to that condition.

Saudi Arabia has not responded to Trump's remarks so far.

White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said on Thursday that Trump had discussed the condition to Saudi leaders on numerous previous occasions.

"He has said if they don't join the Abraham Accords, the deal is off," she said. "So we'll continue to have those conversations with our Saudi counterparts moving forward."

Trump's comments come come after criticism from experts that the US-Saudi deal announced on Wednesday could run the risk of future nuclear proliferation in an already unstable region.

Some Republican and Democratic lawmakers expressed similar concerns, though Trump's Republican Party controls both the upper and lower chambers and opponents of the deal do not appear to have the votes needed to block it.

According to the US Department of Energy, it includes a "peaceful nuclear co-operation agreement" and a "bilateral safeguards agreement".

"Together, these two agreements lay the legal foundation for a decades-long, multi-billion-dollar partnership that advances several priority economic and strategic objectives, including nuclear nonproliferation," a statement from the department said.

It said the agreement would be sent to the US Congress for review.

The International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) said on Thursday that it was aware of US and Saudi plans for a "bilateral civil nuclear cooperation agreement and the plan to ask the IAEA to implement verification measures in relation to this".

"We look forward to receiving the request for such verification and to working with the US and KSA [Kingdom of Saudi Arabia] in ensuring the implementation of those measures."

During Trump's first term as US president, the United Arab Emirates (UAE) and Bahrain signed the Abraham Accords, resulting in a historic normalisation of relations between Israel and two Arab nations - the first in decades.

Sudan, Morocco and Kazakhstan have since also signed them, too.

Saudi Arabia has previously said it will not do so without the establishment of a Palestinian state, but the White House said the deal would be off if Riyadh did not join the accords.

Israel has welcomed Trump's statement.

Posting on X, Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's office said Saudi Arabia joining the accords "would be an historic leap forward for peace in the Middle East".

The statement did not mention the nuclear deal. However, Israeli Economy Minister Nir Barkat said previously his country "can manage it" if Saudi Arabia had nuclear power for peaceful means.

Others were more critical. Former Israeli defence minister Avigdor Lieberman, told the paper: "Every military nuclear program begins with a civilian one."

Israel itself is widely believed to have nuclear weapons, but it neither confirms nor denies that this is the case.

The US-Saudi deal is described as a "peaceful nuclear co-operation agreement" that will provide "great access for American companies in the Saudi nuclear energy programme" by the US energy department.

The nuclear issue has been at the centre of the months-long US-Israeli conflict with Iran which has for years denied Western accusations that it aims to acquire nuclear weapons.

Saudi Arabia's de facto ruler, Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman, told CBS News in 2018 that his own nation was not planning to acquire a nuclear weapon, but that "without a doubt if Iran developed a nuclear bomb, we will follow suit as soon as possible".

Earlier US media reports suggested the deal could allow Saudi Arabia to enrich uranium in the future - which can be used as fuel for power plants, but also potentially to make nuclear bombs. However, Trump's remarks appear to deny this.

Rosemary Kelanic, the director of the Middle East programme at the US-based Defense Priorities think tank, said it would be "quite shocking" if the US were to allow Saudi Arabia uranium enrichment facilities.

"The US has never done that before. We have never helped another country enrich on their own soil," she said, adding that this was because "if you can produce your own nuclear fuel, you become a much bigger risk" for building nuclear weapons.
BreakingNewsTab.description: The deal between the US and Saudi Arabia to help the Gulf kingdom develop nuclear energy is dependent on Riyadh recognising the State of Israel, President Donald Trump has said. "The Civil Nuclear Deal (There will be no enrichment of material!)... which pertains only to non-military use... will be approved, but is...
